coredata.dc:title: Strongly coupled N-doped graphene quantum dots/Ni(Fe)OxHy electrocatalysts with accelerated reaction kinetics for water oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Wanjun
Ning, Baoxing
Xu, Qiucheng
Jiang, Hao
Hu, Yanjie
Li, Chunzhong
coredata.dc:description: Hybridization of metal hydroxides with conductive carbon materials is one of the effective strategies to alleviate their poor electron transfer and improve the slow kinetics of oxygen evolution reaction (OER). However, how to realize the stable and effective fabrication of the composite structure is urgently required but still a challenge. In this work, we constructed a novel electrocatalyst that abundant nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) are uniformly anchored on Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets through the strong M−N−C bonding. The synergistic effect between composite components greatly modifies the metal charge center with higher chemical valence and accelerates the hydroxylation process (*O→*OOH) during OER process. Meanwhile, the smaller apparent activation energy (E a) and faster proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) verify that the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has more favorable reaction kinetics. Consequently, NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y demonstrates an ultralow overpotential of 200 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with a long stability of 36 h. This work provides a viable method to realize the strong combination of carbon materials and metal hydroxides for excellent OER performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalyst
Ni(Fe)O x H y
Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst. (b) SEM image, (c) TEM image of the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . (d) Raman spectra, (e) XPS N 1 s spectra of the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and Ni(Fe)O x H y .
(a) XPS Ni 2p3/2 spectra, (b) XPS Fe 2p3/2 spectra, (c) XRD pattern and (d) OER performance comparison of a series of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalysts with different NGQDs contents.

(a) OER polarization curves, (b) electrochemical double-layer capacitance (C dl), and (c) ECSA-normalized OER polarization curves of RuO2, Ni(Fe)O x H y and NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . (d) Overpotential (η) at jECSA = 1 mA cm−2 and turnover frequency (TOF) at η = 250 mV of Ni(Fe)O x H y and NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . (e) OER performance comparison of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and other reported advanced NiFe-based electrocatalysts. (f) Stability test of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y .

(a) Tafel plots of RuO2, Ni(Fe)O x H y and NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . (b) Nyquist plots, (c) E a and (d) the pH dependence of the Ni2+∼Ni3+ redox peak potential E1 of the Ni(Fe)O x H y and the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y .

(a) XPS Ni 2p3/2 spectra, (b) XPS Fe 2p3/2 spectra, (c) XPS O1s spectra of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and Ni(Fe)O x H y after OER process. (d) schematic diagram of OER enhancement mechanism for NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Strongly coupled N-doped graphene quantum dots/Ni(Fe)OxHy electrocatalysts with accelerated reaction kinetics for water oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education
Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials
School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Hierarchical Nanomaterials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hybridization of metal hydroxides with conductive carbon materials is one of the effective strategies to alleviate their poor electron transfer and improve the slow kinetics of oxygen evolution reaction (OER). However, how to realize the stable and effective fabrication of the composite structure is urgently required but still a challenge. In this work, we constructed a novel electrocatalyst that abundant nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) are uniformly anchored on Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets through the strong M−N−C bonding. The synergistic effect between composite components greatly modifies the metal charge center with higher chemical valence and accelerates the hydroxylation process (*O→*OOH) during OER process. Meanwhile, the smaller apparent activation energy (E a) and faster proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) verify that the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has more favorable reaction kinetics. Consequently, NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y demonstrates an ultralow overpotential of 200 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with a long stability of 36 h. This work provides a viable method to realize the strong combination of carbon materials and metal hydroxides for excellent OER performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Green hydrogen produced by the renewable energy is the key to realize the sustainable energy transformation[1]. Currently, the vast majority of global hydrogen production comes from traditional hydrogen production technologies (e.g. steam reforming, coal gasification, etc.) with high energy consumption. The other only 4% hydrogen is produced by water electrolysis technology, which enables to effectively combine with sustainable clean energy (solar energy, wind energy, etc.) and shows a high energy efficiency[2]. Water electrolysis contains cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and anodic oxygen evolution reaction (OER), in which the OER is normally more sluggish than the HER due to its slow reaction kinetics of proton-coupled four-electron transfers process[3,4]. At present, Ir and Ru-based materials are recognized as the benchmark catalysts for OER, but their scarce reserve seriously restricts the large-scale application[5,6]. Therefore, it is important to develop the earth-abundant transitional metal materials to efficiently produce hydrogen at low potential. Among them, two-dimensional (2D) NiFe layered double hydroxide (NiFe LDH) is the most promising catalyst for the efficient water oxidation[7,8]. Many previous studies have reported that the NiFe LDH would be in-situ converted to highly-active Ni(Fe)O x H y during OER process[9,10]. However, although the newly-formed Ni(Fe)O x H y has a high intrinsic OER activity, its poor electrical conductivity would inevitably hinder the electron transport and speed down the subsequent oxygen intermediates transformation process, resulting a slow reaction kinetics[11–13]. Accordingly, the OER activity of Ni(Fe)O x H y cannot be fully exhibited.

Constructing carbon-based hybrid structure has been widely accepted as one effective strategy to enhance the electrical conductivity of catalysts[12,14,15]. As a co-catalyst, carbon materials can not only accelerate electron transport, but also regulate the electronic structure and chemical stability of metal hydroxides[14]. Particularly, the Fermi energy level difference between metal hydroxides and carbon materials at the heterointerfaces could usually drive the directional flow of valence electrons, thus accelerating the charge transfer process[16,17]. For example, Ma et al. reported the assemble of NiFe LDH with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) through electrostatic interaction, which shows a largely improved conductivity and a faster charge transfer rate[12]. As a result, it exhibits a low overpotential of 210 mV at 10 mA cm−2 with Tafel slope of 40 mV dec−1, much better than that of NiFe LDH (310 mV, 76 mV dec−1). However, the forementioned hybrid catalyst shows a poor stability that it is speculated due to the lack of strong chemical bond interaction between two components, i.e. the NiFe LDH cannot stably anchor on the surface of carbon support. Theoretical and experimental results have reported that the N-doped carbon can form the strong M−N−C bonding with metal-based materials and thus allows to provide a stable composite structure[18]. Besides, fabricating a small amount of carbon materials on transition metal LDH is beneficial to enhance conductivity and simultaneously maintain the high exposure of metal ative sites. Therefore, it is urgently required but still a challenge to construct a composite structure between NiFe LDH and N-doped carbon to realize the fast electron transfer with a maximized exposure of metal active sites for excellent OER performance.

Herein, we constructed a class of novel electrocatalysts that N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were strongly coupled on Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets arrays by the ammonium citrate-assisted hydrothermal method. Surface chemial state analyses indicate that the NGQDs are anchored on Ni(Fe)O x H y through the M−N−C bonding, which leads to a strong electron interaction with the generation of more high-valence metal (Ni3+ and Fe3+). These highly active metal centre sites can effectively promote the hydroxylation transition during OER process, thus extremely improve the OER kinetics. Electrochemical kinetics results further confirm that NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y possesses a small activation energy (E a) and a better proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) process in contrast to pure Ni(Fe)O x H y . Consequently, the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets exhibits excellent electrochemical performance with a ultralow η 10 = 200 mV, a small Tafel slope of 37 mV dec−1 and a long durability for 36 h, among the best reported NiFe-based OER electrocatalysts. This work provides a novel pathway to fabricate the transition metal–carbon composite structure catalysts for excellent water oxidation.

The NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y catalyst was prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.218 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.2 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.2 g of ammonium citrate were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water (DI) and stirred for 30 min. Then, the cleaned nickel foam (NF, 3 cm × 4 cm, thickness of 0.3 mm) was immersed in the above mixed solution and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y was obtained by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 2 h. The as-prepared sample was then washed by DI water and ethyl alcohol for several times. Finally, the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y sample was dried at 80 °C overnight in a vacuum oven. The NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y with different NGQDs contents (0%, 0.7%, 2.0%, 3.4% and 10.2%) were synthesized by adjusting the dosage of ammonium citrate (0 g, 0.02 g, 0.06 g, 0.1 g and 0.3 g). The typical mass loading of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y on NF is ∼ 1.2 mg cm−2. The NGQDs were obtained by direct hydrothermal ammonium citrate. The pure Ni(Fe)O x H y was prepared by the same hydrothermal method except replacing ammonium citrate with sodium citrate.

The structure and morphology of catalyst were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/Max2550, Cu Ka radiation) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) with an X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, S-4800) was applied to obtained the basal morphology of catalyst. The chemical-state of catalyst was determined by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Al Kα X-ray radiation) and Raman spectra (Via + Reflex). Inductively Coupled Plasma-Optical Emission Spectrometer (Agilent 725 ICP-OES) was used to analyze trace elements in samples. A portable UV detector light (UV light, 365 nm) was used to detect the fluorescence reaction of the sample. The ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy (UPS) were recorded with a VG Scienta R4000 analyzer (monochromatic He I light source of 21.2 eV).

Most of electrochemical measurements were tested in 1 M KOH (pH = 14) with a standard three-electrode system through CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments Inc., Shanghai, China). The self-supported electrode (testing area of 0.4 cm × 0.5 cm), saturated Ag/AgCl electrode and graphite electrode were used as the working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The ink of commercial RuO2 powder was prepared by mixing 5 mg of powder, 0.42 mL of DI, 0.5 mL of ethanol and 80 μL of Nafion solution (5 wt%). The ink was drop-coated onto the NF with mass loading of ∼ 1 mg cm−2. The measured potential was calibrated to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potential according to the following equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.1976 + 0.0591 × pH. The polarization curves for OER were measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The stability was measured by chronopotentiometry (CP) at 10 mA cm−2. All data except the CV tested at different temperature were compensated with a 95% IR compensation. The double-layer capacitance (C dl) was obtained through multi-rate CV measurement at scan rates from 10 to 35 mV s−1. The Tafel plots were obtained from voltammetry data, and the Tafel slopes (b) were found by fitting the linear portion of the Tafel plots (η = blog(j) + a), where η is the overpotential and j is the current density. The turnover frequency (TOF) values of the as-prepared electrocatalysts were estimated from the equation: TOF = (J × A) / (4 × n × F), where J is the current density in polarization curves, A is the geometric surface area, F is the Faraday constant (96485C mol−1), and n is the mole number of the active metal sites on the electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements in a frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz were performed at an overpotential of 250 mV by an Autolab PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation. The series resistance (R s) value is equal to the total impedance at the phase angle of 0° in high frequency. The conductivity (σ) is measured by a I-U measurement. The electrocatalysts were clamped with two spacers and then held by a cell clamp. The I-U test was performed in a dry environment using a two-electrode system. In this case, it can be assumed that only the electron transfer process determines the voltage-current response. Therefore, the conductivity (σ) of the electrodes can be found by the formula: σ = ΔI / (ΔU × d), where ΔU is the voltage difference, ΔI is the current difference, and d is the electrode thickness. The apparent activation energy (E a) for water oxidation were calculated by using the Arrhenius relationship: ∂ln(ik) / ∂(1/T) = - E a / (R), where ik is the current density at an overpotential of 300 mV, T is the temperature, and R is the gas constant. Specifically, the CV curves of catalysts at different temperatures were measured without IR compensation. After compiling ln(ik) against 1/T, the slopes of curves are their E a for OER. The Ni2+∼Ni3+redox peak potential (E1) was obtained by testing CV at different pH.

A schematic diagram of the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst is illustrated in Fig. 1
a, where NGQDs are anchored on Ni(Fe)O x H y ultrathin nanosheets with strong electron interaction and they together assembled as arrays structure on NF. Typically, the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y hybrid was prepared by the ammonium citrate-assisted hydrothermal method. The SEM and TEM images of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y are demonstrated in Fig. 1 b, 1c and
Fig. S1 . The interlaced nanosheets with thickness of ∼ 20 nm are orderly arranged as arrays structure on NF to form an integral and self-supported electrode. Further observation shows that abundant NGQDs with size of 2 to 4.5 nm are uniformly anchored on the amorphous Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets. The inset shows a single NGQD that its interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the hexagonal (110) of carbon[19]. The XPS O 1 s spectrum ( Fig. S2 ) shows that the main O peak is –OH content, suggesting the existence form of Ni and Fe in electrocatalysts are NiFe hydroxides. The SEM-EDS mapping of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y ( Fig. S3 ) exhibits a uniform distribution of Ni, Fe, C, N and O on the nanosheet, indicating the formation of composites. Such composite structure is also clarified by the Raman spectra (Fig. 1 d and Fig. S4 ) that NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y contains two groups of characteristic peaks. It shows that two peaks below 600 nm−1 assign to the eg bending vibration (δ) and A1g stretching vibration (ν) of NiIII-O[20,21]. The other two peaks at 1426 and 1602 nm−1 correspond to the defective band (D) and graphitized band (G) of carbon materials, respectively[22]. By comparing these carbon peaks with the pure NGQDs ( Fig. S4 ), it shows a blue shift to higher wavenumbers, indicating the atomic configuration change of carbon with higher graphitization degree. Moreover, the XPS N 1 s spectrum of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y is shown in Fig. 1 e. Except the characteristic peaks of N-doped carbon such as Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N and Graphitic-N, it also shows a peak at 399.5 eV assigned to the M−N−C bonds (M: Ni or Fe), which manifests the strong chemical bonding between NGQDs and Ni(Fe)O x H y
[23–25]. All above results suggest the formation of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y composite structure during one-step synthesis process.

To further exploit the formation mechanism of NGQDs loaded on the Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets, contrast experiments were insightfully designed. Interestingly, it was found that the pure Ni(Fe)O x H y was prepared if ammonium citrate was substituted to sodium citrate during hydrothermal process. Raman spectra of as-prepared Ni(Fe)O x H y were shown in Fig. 1 d with no carbon characteristic peaks and its nanosheet structure in TEM image ( Fig. S5 ) shows a smooth surface without any loadings. Ultraviolet fluorescence projection test ( Fig. S6 ) also implies that there are no NGQDs formation. These results indicate the ammonium (NH4 +) plays a significant function on the formation of NGQDs. We proposed that the N inside ammonium can be used as anchor knots for looping the carbon chain of citrate and thus induces the formation of NGQDs during synthesis[26]. Accordingly, the direct hydrothermal process of ammonium citrate was tentatively implemented. As expected, pure NGQDs were successfully obtained. Their basic characterizations are shown in Fig. S7 . TEM and AFM images exhibit the NGQDs are 2 to 4.5 nm in size with thickness of ∼ 1.1 nm [27]. And its disperse solution shows the characteristic phenomenon of GQDs with an obvious bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation[26,28]. The N doping of these GQD is further proved by the XPS full spectrum. It has been reported that N-doped GQDs enrich N-dangling bonds and thus they can provide the potential junction sites for anchoring the metal centres of support materials. In conclusion, the ammonium citrate can not only serve as nitrogen source to form the N-doped GQDs but also helps to create rich M−N−C bonds for tightly linking Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets.

To investigate the electron interaction between the composite components, a series of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalysts with different NGQDs contents ranging from 0% to 10.2% were controllably prepared by changing the amount of ammonium citrate. The NGQDs contents were estimated based on the EDS and XPS results. Fig. 2
a exhibits the Ni 2p3/2 spectra of different NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . All samples possess two metal peaks (Ni2+ and Ni3+) and one satellite peak[31]. By comparing with pure Ni(Fe)O x H y sample (0% NGQDs), other samples show an obvious positive shift (∼1.3 eV) of Ni2+ metal peaks, indicating the strong electron interaction between Ni(Fe)O x H y and NGQDs. It has also been reported high-valence metal ions are conducive to the hydroxylation transition and thus can improve the OER kinetics[30]. Moreover, the UPS spectra and derived work functions of two contrast samples ( Fig. S8 ) shows the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has a work function of 1.2 eV lower than that of Ni(Fe)O x H y (1.3 eV), suggesting its higher Fermi level energy and faster electron transfer efficiency. This electron structure improvement may be attributed to the interfacial electronic interaction through the M−N−C bonds. Accordingly, the Ni3+/Ni2+ ratios of all samples were calculated by dividing their peak areas. With the increase of NGQDs content, their Ni3+/Ni2+ ratios firstly increase and then reach to the maximum at NGQDs content of 6.8%. Further raise of NGQDs content cannot increase the Ni3+/Ni2+ ratio, which may be due to too strong electron effect affecting the nucleation growth of Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets. Identically, the highest Fe3+/Fe2+ ratio in Fe 2p3/2 spectra (Fig. 2 b) also appears to the NGQDs 6.8% sample [29,31]. Moreover, SEM images of these samples are provided in Fig. S9 , which all show a nanosheet morphology. In their XRD pattern (Fig. 2 c), only characteristic peaks of NF are presented, indicating the formation of amorphous Ni(Fe)O x H y , which agrees with the TEM results. Also, no carbon peaks can be detected due to the small particle size and low content of NGQDs. The OER performance of these hybrid electrocatalysts were further explored. Fig. S10 exhibits the polarization curves and Tafel slopes of all samples. Among them, 6.8% NGQDs sample has the best OER activity and fastest reaction rate. Fig. 2 d summarizes their overpotentials at different current densities. It shows the 6.8% NGQDs sample only requires overpotential of 200 and 227 mV to reach 10 and 100 mA cm−2, much better than those of the pure Ni(Fe)O x H y and other samples. Besides, the influence of Ni and Fe ratios in catalyst on OER performance were also explored. A series of electrocatalysts were prepared by adjusting the feed ratio of Ni and Fe precursors. The Ni/Fe ratios of the as-prepared composites is determined by ICP-OES (Table S1). Fig. S11 shows their LSV curves, in which the sample with Ni/Fe ratio of 1.5 has the best OER performance. The above-mentioned regulation of NGQDs contents is based on this screened sample. Therefore, the optimized NGQDs content of 6.8% for OER is screened out. Above results illuminate that the combination of NGQDs can largely modify the electron structure of Ni(Fe)O x H y and a suitable NGQDs content optimizes the OER performance[17].

To highlight the key role of carbon-based composite structure for improving the OER performance, Fig. 3
a demonstrates the polarization curves of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and Ni(Fe)O x H y measured in 1 M KOH electrolyte. The NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y only needs a small overpotential of 200 mV to reach 10 mA cm−2, superior to those of pure Ni(Fe)O x H y (223 mV) and benchmark RuO2 (256 mV). As mentioned above, the pure Ni(Fe)O x H y was prepared by using sodium citrate, which has a similar nanosheet arrays structure as the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . By applying the multi-sweep cycling CV method ( Fig. S12 ), the double-layer capacitances (C dl) of two contrast samples are obtained, as shown in Fig. 3 b. It shows Ni(Fe)O x H y has a C dl value of 16.0 mF cm−2 very close to that of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y of 16.9 mF cm−2, indicating that their active surface areas are relatively identical. Therefore, it can be assumed that the improvement of OER performance for NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y comes from the synergistic effect of composite components for enhancing intrinsic activity. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) normalized polarization curves are presented in Fig. 3 c to reflect the intrinsic activity of different samples. Specifically, the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has an overpotential improvement of 109.7 mV in contrast to Ni(Fe)O x H y at j ECSA = 1 mA cm−2. The TOF value of two sample (Fig. 3 d) is further calculated by normalizing the total number of electrochemically accessible Ni active sites. At η = 250 mV, NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y exhibits a larger TOF value of 0.18 s−1 than that of Ni(Fe)O x H y (0.06 s−1), which suggests the better intrinsic activity of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y . Moreover, Fig. 3 e exhibits the OER performance comparison of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and other recently reported advanced NiFe-based electrocatalysts[32–38]. Among them, NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst possesses the lowest overpotential and Tafel slope, standing out its excellent OER performance. The catalytic stability of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y was measured by CP tests. In Fig. 3 f, it shows the negligible potential and morphology change (inset) at j = 10 mA cm−2 during the 36 h testing. Furthermore, it also shows a small degradation rate of 1.5 mV h−1 at large current density of 100 mA cm−2 for 24 h ( Fig. S13 ), suggesting its excellent durability for OER catalysis.

The water oxidation kinetics of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst is further investigated. Fig. 4
a and 4b show the Tafel slopes and Nyquist plots of two contrast samples, in which the NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has both smaller Tafel slope of 37 mV dec−1 and R ct of 7.06 Ω. The fitting results of Nyquist plots were shown in Table S2. Both results reflect the faster OER kinetics of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y , suggesting that the combination of NGQDs enables to improve electronic conductivity ( Fig. S14 ) and accelerate the charge transfer process of Ni(Fe)O x H y , and thus extremely improve the rate-determining step for OER catalysis[39]. The polarization curves of two samples ( Fig. S15 ) were further measured at different temperatures to achieve the Arrhenius plot, whose slopes represent the E a of catalysts. Generally, a more rapid OER rate appearing at an elevated temperature reflects the temperature dependence of the chemical rate constant[40]. Fig. 4 c exhibits the E a of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and Ni(Fe)O x H y . It has been reported that a smaller E a is beneficial to reduce the binding energy of OER intermediates[41,42]. The NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has a small E a of 23.7 kJ mol−1, lower than that of Ni(Fe)O x H y (30.3 kJ mol−1), indicating that the modified NGQDs could optimize the intermediates adsorption on Ni(Fe)O x H y during OER process. Moreover, the polarization curves of two samples at different pH values ( Fig. S16 ) are also tested. The pH dependence of the Ni2+∼Ni3+redox peak potential (E1) is obtained, as shown in Fig. 4 d. Along with the increase of pH value, the E 1 peaks of both samples show a negative shift. The relationship between peak position and pH value can be explained by proton coupled electron transfer (PCET)[43]. Normally, electron removal is associated with proton shift in many hydroxides and it also increases the valence of the catalytic metal centres[44]. PCET therefore is important not only for the in-situ formation of active sites in redox process, but also for the localization of holes/radicals in charge transfer. A larger E1 peaks-pH value slope (k) reflects a better the PECT process. NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y possesses a larger k of 0.06, confirming that the introduction of carbon-based materials can effectively make up for the slow reaction kinetics of Ni(Fe)O x H y for OER intermediates transition (steps involve electron transfer). It has been reported the wettability of catalyst can affect the desorption rate of bubbles from the electrode surface during OER process. Accordingly, the wettability of two electrocatalyst were measured, as shown in Fig. S17 . The NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y has a smaller contact angle of 62° compared by the Ni(Fe)O x H y of 95°, indicating its better wettability for accelerating bubble-releasing process. Besides, the Faradaic efficiency of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst was also measured by drainage method at 100 mA cm−2 ( Fig. S18 and Table S3). By comparing the measured O2 with the theoretical value, it can be calculated that the Faradaic efficiency of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y is close to 100%. As a result, it can be concluded that the superior OER performance of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst origins from the synergistic effect between two components for improving OER kinetics.

The surface chemical state of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y and Ni(Fe)O x H y after OER process is also analysed. Fig. 5
a and 5b exhibit the XPS Ni 2p3/2 and Fe 2p3/2 spectra, which compare the Ni3+/Ni2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ ratios of two contrast samples. They show that the introduction of carbon materials greatly increases the Ni3+/Ni2+ and Fe3+/Fe2+ ratios of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y , suggesting more catalytically active sites (normally the M3+–OOH components) are formed on NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y surface. This is further verified by the XPS O 1 s spectra (Fig. 5 c), where more –OOH active components (55.9%) is formed on the surface of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y much higher than that of pure Ni(Fe)O x H y (30.1%). It has been reported the –OOH active components is the key reactant in the final step of oxygen release and therefore it plays a significant role in determining the OER rate[30,45]. The higher valence ratios and more –OOH active components of NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y indicate that the O2 release process (*OOH + OH– → O2 + H2O + *) is favourable to occur on its surface. Finally, the schematic diagram of OER enhancement mechanism for NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y electrocatalyst is illustrated in Fig. 5 d. The introduction of NGQDs can not only enhance the conductivity of catalyst for improving OER kinetics (Fig. 4 a and 4b) but also dramatically modify the electron structure of Ni(Fe)O x H y for optimizing the intermediates adsorption (Fig. 4 c and 4d). Particularly, electrons would directionally flow from Ni(Fe)O x H y to NGQDs after forming the composite structure, resulting in the positively charged metal centre and negatively charged NGQDs. The positively charged metal centres promote hydroxylation transition with the formation of abundant –OOH active components. These highly-active catalytic sites optimize the intermediates adsorption (*O and *OOH) and thus enhance the OER kinetics and intrinsic activity. Some related theoretical calculations in previous literature can also support our understanding on reaction mechanism enhancement[46].

In summary, a novel composite electrocatalyst that comprised by abundant NGQDs anchored on Ni(Fe)O x H y nanosheets is successfully synthesized by ammonium citrate-assisted hydrothermal method. The electronic interaction between NGQDs and Ni(Fe)O x H y was well-modified by regulating the NGQDs content. The optimized hybrid catalyst with NGQDs content of ∼ 6.8 at% is beneficial to enhance conductivity and simultaneously maintain the high exposure of metal active sites, resulting in an excellent OER performance (η10 = 200 mV). Further electrochemical measurements combining with the surface chemical state analyses show that NGQDs/Ni(Fe)O x H y possesses a lower E a, a faster PECT and more surface active components, verifying the synergetic effect between NGQDs and Ni(Fe)O x H y can optimize the intermediates adsorption (*O and *OOH) for a much enhanced reaction kinetics. This work provides a feasible perspective for the realization of strongly coupling carbon materials and metal hydroxides as OER electrocatalysts to improve activity and stability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21838003 and 51621002), the Shanghai Scientific and Technological Innovation Project (18JC1410600), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201718002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.133068.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
